source_take: A common take: 'High staking APY means this coin is a good investment — more rewards equals more value'
content:
SLIDE 1: A common take: 'high staking APY = good investment'

SLIDE 2: game theory sees something different. high APY exists to prevent selling — it's a coordination mechanism, not a value signal.

SLIDE 3: if every holder is locked into staking rewards, supply looks stable. but what happens when the rewards drop?

SLIDE 4: the model scores this differently — APY alone is a coordination trap, not a competitive moat.

SLIDE 5: what to look for instead: does a Nash equilibrium hold even without the APY incentive? that's the real signal.

SLIDE 6: full rankings at nashsatoshi.com — link in bio [product dashboard screenshot]

CAPTION: pov: the metric everyone uses as a buy signal is actually a warning flag

HASHTAGS: #crypto, #AIcrypto, #cryptoanalysis, #gametheory

content:
TITLE: I've been building a crypto ranking system based on game theory instead of price action — here's the methodology and what surprised me

BODY:
Most crypto analysis is backward-looking. Technical analysis tells you where price has been. Momentum tracking tells you what's moving right now. Neither tells you what a coin is positioned to do.

I've been experimenting with a different approach — applying game theory to crypto positioning. Instead of asking "where is this going," the framework asks: what are the Nash equilibria for everyone involved — holders, validators, protocols, and speculators?

Four dimensions the model scores on:

**Coordination dynamics** — does the coin have a mechanism that makes holding rational even when price drops?

**Narrative positioning** — is the story strong enough to sustain liquidity without a price catalyst?

**Liquidity incentive structure** — who benefits from keeping the market liquid, and does that incentive hold when conditions change?

**Protocol-level game theory** — what happens if a significant portion of validators defect? Does the system hold?

The output is a ranking based on structural positioning, not price prediction.

What surprised me most: meme coins score higher on coordination dynamics than most "fundamentals" coins. The community Nash equilibrium is actually quite stable when you model it correctly — the crowd makes fun of meme coin believers but the game theory of why they hold is more rational than most TA-based analysis gives it credit for.

Curious if anyone else is approaching analysis this way or if there's prior research I'm missing. Also interested in what dimensions people think are most important that I might be leaving out.

[Flair: Discussion]
score_notes: Hook technique: Counterintuitive advice (do the opposite — game theory vs TA). Genuine value-first post. Nash Satoshi not linked or pushed — let it come up naturally in comments. Flair: Discussion. ⚠️ Check karma before posting.

took longer to understand Apple's review guidelines than to build the app.

Vista tracks what you've watched, builds your taste profile over time.

the code was the easy part.
the App Store submission process has layers nobody warns you about.

if you're building anything for iOS — more than happy to share what I ran into.

content:
Letterboxd is a social network pretending to be a movie tracker.

your ratings are shaped by what you see other people rating.
your lists are public performances, not personal data.

Vista doesn't have a feed. just you, your ratings, and your taste profile.

the data you build is yours — not content for a platform.
score_notes: Hook technique: One-liner gut punch. DEBATE HOOK — will attract pushback from Letterboxd fans. Defensible claim about social dynamics of public rating platforms. Grounded in Vista's actual differentiator (no social feed).

content:
crowd ratings answer 'is this good.'
personal taste profiles answer 'is this good for me.'

IMDb and RT average out millions of opinions — critics, casual viewers, people who hated the director.

useful for cultural consensus.
useless for predicting what you'll think.

after enough films logged in Vista, the recommendations stop being generic.

available on the App Store: https://apps.apple.com/us/app/vista-movie-taste-profiles/id6758186885

content:
TITLE: Been tracking every film I watch in an app I built — curious if others who log films notice the same gap between their personal ratings and the aggregates

BODY:
Started logging every film I watch in a personal tracking app I built called Vista — it builds a taste profile rather than publishing ratings publicly like Letterboxd.

A few weeks in, some patterns in my own data that I didn't expect:

**Genre isn't the predictor I thought.** I rate sci-fi highly on average, but some of my lowest-rated films are sci-fi. The more accurate predictor for me seems to be pacing and narrative ambiguity — films with those elements I consistently rate higher, regardless of genre.

**Oscars attention doesn't move my ratings.** The films getting awards attention right now don't correlate with what I've personally rated highest this year. Made me wonder how many people would find the same gap if they actually tracked their preferences vs. what they "should" like.

**Cult films score higher for me than the aggregates suggest.** Midsommar is a 7.1 on IMDb. I'd put it well above that. The crowdsourced average compresses a huge range of reactions — horror fans, casual viewers, people who walked out — into one number that doesn't serve any specific viewer's actual taste.

Curious if others who actively track what they watch notice similar patterns — does your personal data match what aggregate ratings suggest about you, or are there consistent gaps?

[Flair: Discussion]
score_notes: Hook technique: Counterintuitive advice implied (track your own data vs. trust aggregates). Value-first Reddit post. Vista mentioned naturally once as context, not promoted. No link. ⚠️ Check karma before posting. Flair: Discussion.
